subject: Statistics
title: Simpson's Paradox in Regression
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Simpson's Paradox in Regression.

hook.text: A drug helps men and women separately, but hurts the combined population. How is that possible?

buildup.text: Simpson's paradox occurs when a trend reverses after combining groups. The aggregate tells a different story.

discovery.text: It happens when a lurking variable is unevenly distributed. More sick women took the drug, dragging down the average.

twist.text: Without controlling for the lurking variable, the regression coefficient points the wrong direction entirely.

climax.text: The lesson: always consider what's being hidden by aggregation. Pooled data can lie.

punchline.text: Combine the groups and the truth can reverse.

quiz.question: What causes Simpson's paradox?
options: A lurking variable is unevenly distributed across groups, reversing the trend when combined
The sample size is too small
The regression model has too many predictors
